skills.teks_description: Graph and write the inverse of a function using notation such as f^-1(x)
skills.prompt: This topic covers advanced functions for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.2(B)). Students should graph and write inverses of functions. Finding inverses: swap x and y, solve for y; f^(-1)(x) notation. Graphical relationship: inverse is reflection over y = x. Conditions: only one-to-one functions have inverses (pass horizontal line test); restrict domain to make non-one-to-one functions invertible. Composition verification: f(f^(-1)(x)) = x and f^(-1)(f(x)) = x. Advanced transformations: combining multiple transformations (order matters), transformations of absolute value, rational, exponential, and logarithmic functions. Piecewise functions: defined by different rules on different intervals; continuity and differentiability at boundary points. Vocabulary: inverse function, one-to-one, horizontal line test, composition, domain restriction, piecewise function, transformation, reflection, parent function.

skills.skill: Polynomial functions (division, remainder theorem, factor theorem)
skills.teks_description: Determine quotients of polynomials divided by linear or quadratic polynomials
skills.prompt: This topic covers polynomial division for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.7(C)). Students should find quotients of polynomials divided by linear or quadratic divisors. Long division: systematic process for dividing any polynomial by any polynomial of equal or lesser degree. Synthetic division: efficient shortcut when dividing by (x - c). Remainder theorem: when polynomial f(x) is divided by (x - c), the remainder equals f(c). Factor theorem: (x - c) is a factor of f(x) if and only if f(c) = 0. Finding zeros: use rational root theorem (possible rational zeros = ±factors of constant / factors of leading coefficient), test candidates using synthetic division, then factor the reduced polynomial. Polynomial inequalities: find zeros, test intervals, determine sign. Key concepts: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, relationship between factors and zeros, writing polynomials from given zeros, Descartes' Rule of Signs. Vocabulary: polynomial division, quotient, remainder, synthetic division, Remainder Theorem, Factor Theorem, rational root theorem, zero, factor, Descartes' Rule of Signs.

skills.teks_description: Determine asymptotic restrictions and represent domain/range using notation and set theory
skills.prompt: This topic covers advanced rational function analysis for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.6(K)). Students should perform complete analysis including asymptotic behavior, domain, and range. Advanced topics: oblique (slant) asymptotes when numerator degree exceeds denominator degree by 1 (found by polynomial division), holes vs. vertical asymptotes (factor and simplify), end behavior analysis. Graphing strategy: (1) factor and simplify, (2) find domain restrictions, (3) identify vertical asymptotes and holes, (4) determine horizontal or oblique asymptote, (5) find intercepts, (6) determine sign behavior in each interval. Solving rational equations: find LCD, multiply through, solve, check for extraneous solutions (values that make original denominator zero). Rational inequalities: find critical values (zeros and restrictions), test intervals, include endpoints where ≤ or ≥ (but never include restrictions). Applications: inverse variation, average rate problems, mixture problems. Vocabulary: rational function, vertical asymptote, horizontal asymptote, oblique asymptote, hole, domain, range, end behavior, extraneous solution, interval notation.

skills.teks_description: Generate and solve logarithmic equations in mathematical and real-world problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers exponential and logarithmic applications for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(H)). Students should generate and solve logarithmic equations in real-world contexts. Applications of exponential functions: population growth P(t) = P0e^(rt), radioactive decay N(t) = N0e^(-λt), compound interest A = P(1+r/n)^(nt), continuous compounding A = Pe^(rt), Newton's law of cooling T(t) = Ts + (T0-Ts)e^(-kt). Applications of logarithmic functions: pH = -log[H+], Richter scale M = log(I/I0), decibel scale dB = 10log(I/I0), doubling time t = ln(2)/r, half-life t = ln(2)/λ. Solving techniques: isolate exponential/log expression, apply inverse operation (log or exponentiation), use properties of logarithms to simplify. Key concepts: logarithmic scales compress large ranges, exponential models describe many natural phenomena, choosing between exponential and linear models based on data. Vocabulary: exponential growth, exponential decay, half-life, doubling time, compound interest, continuous compounding, logarithmic scale, pH, decibel, Newton's law of cooling.

skills.skill: Trigonometric functions (law of sines, law of cosines)
skills.teks_description: Use the Law of Sines in mathematical and real-world problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers the law of sines and law of cosines for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.4(G)). Students should solve oblique (non-right) triangles. Law of Sines: a/sin(A) = b/sin(B) = c/sin(C); used when given AAS, ASA, or SSA (ambiguous case). Law of Cosines: c² = a² + b² - 2ab·cos(C); used when given SAS or SSS. Ambiguous case (SSA): zero, one, or two triangles may be possible; compare the given side with the altitude to determine. Area formulas: A = ½ab·sin(C) using two sides and included angle; Heron's formula A = √(s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)) where s = (a+b+c)/2. Applications: navigation, surveying, force triangles, distance problems with non-right angles. Problem-solving strategy: determine what information is given, choose appropriate law, solve systematically. Vocabulary: oblique triangle, Law of Sines, Law of Cosines, ambiguous case, included angle, Heron's formula, bearing, angle of elevation, angle of depression.

skills.teks_description: Use trigonometric identities including reciprocal, quotient, Pythagorean, sum and difference identities
skills.prompt: This topic covers advanced trigonometric identities for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(M)). Students should apply sum, difference, double-angle, and half-angle identities. Sum and difference: sin(A±B) = sinA·cosB ± cosA·sinB, cos(A±B) = cosA·cosB ∓ sinA·sinB, tan(A±B) = (tanA ± tanB)/(1 ∓ tanA·tanB). Double-angle: sin(2A) = 2sinA·cosA, cos(2A) = cos²A - sin²A = 2cos²A - 1 = 1 - 2sin²A, tan(2A) = 2tanA/(1 - tan²A). Half-angle: sin(A/2) = ±√((1-cosA)/2), cos(A/2) = ±√((1+cosA)/2). Applications: simplifying expressions, solving equations, evaluating exact values of non-standard angles (e.g., cos(15°) using cos(45°-30°)). Product-to-sum and sum-to-product formulas for advanced applications. Vocabulary: sum identity, difference identity, double-angle identity, half-angle identity, product-to-sum, verify, simplify, exact value.

skills.teks_description: Represent the addition of vectors and multiplication of a vector by a scalar geometrically and symbolically
skills.prompt: This topic covers vector operations for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.4(J)). Students should represent vector addition and scalar multiplication geometrically and symbolically. Vector operations: addition v + w = <v1+w1, v2+w2>, subtraction, scalar multiplication cv = <cv1, cv2>. Magnitude: |v| = √(v1² + v2²). Unit vector: v/|v|. Dot product: v · w = v1w1 + v2w2 = |v||w|cos θ; scalar result; used to find angle between vectors, determine orthogonality (dot product = 0), find projections. Vector projection: proj_w(v) = ((v·w)/(w·w))w. Cross product (3D): v × w = <v2w3-v3w2, v3w1-v1w3, v1w2-v2w1>; vector result perpendicular to both inputs; magnitude |v||w|sin θ = area of parallelogram. Applications: work (W = F·d), torque (τ = r × F), navigation, physics problems. Vocabulary: vector, scalar, magnitude, direction, component, unit vector, dot product, cross product, projection, orthogonal, parallel, resultant.

skills.teks_description: Solve systems using Gaussian elimination, matrices, or substitution
skills.prompt: This topic covers matrix operations for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.3(B)). Students should use matrices to solve systems and understand matrix algebra. Operations: addition/subtraction (same dimensions), scalar multiplication, matrix multiplication (rows by columns). Determinant: 2×2 det = ad-bc; 3×3 by cofactor expansion; det = 0 means matrix is singular (no inverse). Inverse: A^(-1) exists when det(A) ≠ 0; for 2×2: A^(-1) = (1/det)[d,-b;-c,a]; solving systems AX = B gives X = A^(-1)B. Gaussian elimination: augment [A|I], row reduce to [I|A^(-1)]. Properties: (AB)^(-1) = B^(-1)A^(-1), det(AB) = det(A)det(B). Cramer's rule: solve systems using determinants. Key concepts: matrix representation of linear transformations, rotation and reflection matrices. Vocabulary: matrix, determinant, inverse, singular, nonsingular, augmented matrix, row echelon form, Gaussian elimination, Cramer's rule, identity matrix, cofactor, adjugate.

skills.teks_description: Formulate systems of equations including three linear equations in three variables
skills.prompt: This topic covers systems of equations using matrices for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.3(A)). Students should formulate and solve systems of three linear equations in three unknowns. Representing systems: coefficient matrix, variable matrix, constant matrix; AX = B. Solving methods: substitution (solve one equation for one variable, substitute into others), elimination (add/subtract equations to eliminate variables), Gaussian elimination (row reduce augmented matrix to echelon form), matrix inverse method (X = A^(-1)B), Cramer's rule (using determinants). Solution types: unique solution (planes intersect at a point), no solution (inconsistent — parallel planes), infinitely many solutions (dependent — planes share a line or are identical). Applications: mixture problems, cost problems, network flow, balancing chemical equations, fitting curves through points. Key concepts: geometric interpretation in 3D (each equation represents a plane), identifying solution type from row-reduced matrix, parametric solutions for dependent systems. Vocabulary: system of equations, three variables, augmented matrix, echelon form, Gaussian elimination, consistent, inconsistent, dependent, independent, parameter, plane, intersection.

skills.teks_description: Use the characteristics of a hyperbola to write the equation of a hyperbola with center (h,k)
skills.prompt: This topic covers advanced conic sections for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.3(I)). Students should write and analyze equations of hyperbolas and all conics with center (h,k). Hyperbola: (x-h)²/a² - (y-k)²/b² = 1 (horizontal transverse axis) or (y-k)²/a² - (x-h)²/b² = 1 (vertical). Features: center (h,k), vertices (±a from center along transverse axis), foci (±c from center, c² = a² + b²), asymptotes y-k = ±(b/a)(x-h) or ±(a/b)(x-h). General second-degree equation: Ax² + Bxy + Cy² + Dx + Ey + F = 0; discriminant B²-4AC determines type (negative = ellipse/circle, zero = parabola, positive = hyperbola). Converting general to standard form: complete the square for x and y terms. Rotation of conics: when B ≠ 0, axes are rotated; rotation angle θ = ½ arctan(B/(A-C)). Eccentricity: e = c/a; circle (e=0), ellipse (0<e<1), parabola (e=1), hyperbola (e>1). Vocabulary: hyperbola, transverse axis, conjugate axis, asymptote, eccentricity, focus, vertex, general form, standard form, rotation of axes, discriminant, degenerate conic.

skills.teks_description: Calculate the nth term and partial sum of geometric series and sum of infinite geometric series when it exists
skills.prompt: This topic covers convergence and divergence for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(E)). Students should analyze geometric series for convergence. Geometric series: Σ(ar^n); finite sum Sn = a(1-r^n)/(1-r); infinite sum S = a/(1-r) only when |r| < 1 (convergent). When |r| ≥ 1, the series diverges (sum grows without bound or oscillates). nth term: an = a1 × r^(n-1). Partial sums: sum of first n terms; sequence of partial sums determines convergence. Key concepts: convergence means partial sums approach a finite limit, divergence means they do not; geometric series is the foundational model for convergence analysis. Applications: repeating decimals as infinite geometric series (0.333... = 1/3), present value of annuities, drug dosing models, fractal geometry (Koch snowflake perimeter), bouncing ball total distance. Telescoping series: consecutive terms cancel, leaving finite sum. Vocabulary: sequence, series, partial sum, convergent, divergent, geometric series, common ratio, infinite series, limit, sum to infinity, telescoping series.

skills.teks_description: Determine various types of discontinuities in the interval as they relate to functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers introduction to limits for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(L)). Students should understand various types of discontinuities and the concept of limits. Limit definition: the value f(x) approaches as x approaches a value c; written lim(x→c) f(x) = L. Types of discontinuities: removable (hole — limit exists but function value differs or is undefined), jump (left and right limits exist but differ), infinite (function approaches ±∞, vertical asymptote). One-sided limits: lim(x→c⁻) and lim(x→c⁺); two-sided limit exists only when both one-sided limits exist and are equal. Evaluating limits: direct substitution (when function is continuous), factoring and simplifying (for 0/0 forms), rationalizing (multiply by conjugate), graphical and numerical (table of values). Limits at infinity: horizontal asymptotes; for rational functions, compare degrees of numerator and denominator. Key concepts: limits describe behavior near a point (not necessarily at it), continuity requires f(c) = lim(x→c) f(x), indeterminate forms (0/0, ∞/∞). Vocabulary: limit, continuity, discontinuity, removable, jump, infinite, one-sided limit, indeterminate form, horizontal asymptote, behavior, approach.

skills.teks_description: Analyze and describe end behavior of functions using infinity notation
skills.prompt: This topic covers introduction to derivatives for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(J)). Students should analyze end behavior of functions and understand the derivative as a rate of change. Derivative concept: instantaneous rate of change; slope of tangent line at a point; f'(x) = lim(h→0) [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h. Basic rules: power rule d/dx(x^n) = nx^(n-1), constant rule d/dx(c) = 0, constant multiple rule d/dx(cf) = cf', sum/difference rule d/dx(f±g) = f'±g'. Product rule: d/dx(fg) = f'g + fg'. Quotient rule: d/dx(f/g) = (f'g - fg')/g². Chain rule: d/dx(f(g(x))) = f'(g(x))·g'(x). End behavior: analyze lim(x→±∞) to determine horizontal asymptotes and long-term trends. Key concepts: derivative as slope function, relationship between function and its derivative, where derivative equals zero (critical points), positive derivative means increasing function, negative means decreasing. Vocabulary: derivative, instantaneous rate of change, tangent line, slope, power rule, product rule, quotient rule, chain rule, critical point, increasing, decreasing.

skills.skill: Applications of derivatives (rates of change, optimization)
skills.teks_description: Determine and analyze key features of functions including domain, range, zeros, relative max/min
skills.prompt: This topic covers derivative applications for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(I)). Students should determine key features of functions including domain, range, zeros, and extrema. First derivative test: find critical points (f'(x) = 0 or undefined), test sign changes to identify local maxima and minima. Second derivative test: f''(x) > 0 at critical point means local min, f''(x) < 0 means local max. Optimization: identify quantity to maximize/minimize, express as function of one variable, find critical points, verify maximum/minimum, answer in context. Related rates: identify changing quantities, write equation relating them, differentiate with respect to time, substitute known values, solve. Applications: velocity and acceleration (v = ds/dt, a = dv/dt), marginal cost/revenue, population growth rate, optimization of area, volume, profit, cost. Key concepts: concavity (f'' > 0 concave up, f'' < 0 concave down), inflection points, interpreting derivative graphs. Vocabulary: optimization, critical point, local maximum, local minimum, absolute maximum, absolute minimum, related rates, concavity, inflection point, marginal, velocity, acceleration.

skills.teks_description: Evaluate finite sums and geometric series written in sigma notation
skills.prompt: This topic covers introduction to integrals for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(A)). Students should evaluate finite sums and develop understanding of integration as accumulation. Sigma notation: Σ represents summation; Σ(i=1 to n) f(i) = f(1) + f(2) + ... + f(n). Summation formulas: Σi = n(n+1)/2, Σi² = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6, Σi³ = [n(n+1)/2]². Riemann sums: approximate area under curve by dividing into rectangles (left, right, midpoint); more rectangles = better approximation. Definite integral: ∫[a,b] f(x)dx = lim(n→∞) Σf(xi)Δx; represents exact area under curve. Fundamental Theorem of Calculus: ∫[a,b] f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a) where F'(x) = f(x). Basic antiderivatives: ∫x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C (n≠-1), ∫1/x dx = ln|x| + C, ∫e^x dx = e^x + C. Applications: area between curves, displacement from velocity, total accumulation. Vocabulary: integral, antiderivative, Riemann sum, sigma notation, area under curve, definite integral, indefinite integral, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, accumulation, constant of integration.

skills.teks_description: Explain how sample statistics and confidence levels combine to construct confidence intervals
skills.prompt: This topic covers advanced probability and statistics for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS S.6(A)). Students should understand confidence intervals and their construction. Confidence intervals: range of values likely containing the population parameter; CI = point estimate ± margin of error. For population mean (σ known): x̄ ± z*(σ/√n). Common confidence levels: 90% (z* = 1.645), 95% (z* = 1.96), 99% (z* = 2.576). Components: sample statistic (point estimate), critical value (z* or t*), standard error (σ/√n or s/√n). Increasing confidence level widens the interval; increasing sample size narrows it. Interpretation: we are C% confident that the true population parameter falls within the interval. t-distribution: used when σ is unknown and n is small; approaches normal as n increases. Hypothesis testing introduction: null and alternative hypotheses, p-values, Type I and Type II errors. Vocabulary: confidence interval, margin of error, critical value, standard error, sample size, confidence level, point estimate, t-distribution, hypothesis test, p-value, null hypothesis, significance level.

skills.teks_description: Examine scatterplots to identify patterns, linearity, outliers, and influential points
skills.prompt: This topic covers data analysis and regression for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS S.7(A)). Students should examine scatterplots to identify patterns, linearity, outliers, and influential points. Scatterplot analysis: identify direction (positive, negative, none), form (linear, quadratic, exponential), strength (strong, moderate, weak), unusual features (outliers, clusters, gaps). Linear regression: least-squares regression line ŷ = a + bx minimizes sum of squared residuals; b = r(sy/sx), a = ȳ - bx̄. Residual analysis: residual = observed - predicted; residual plot should show random scatter for good fit; patterns in residuals suggest model inadequacy. Correlation coefficient r: measures linear association strength and direction; r² represents proportion of variance explained. Influential points: high leverage points (extreme x-values) and outliers can dramatically affect regression line. Multiple regression introduction: ŷ = a + b1x1 + b2x2 for multiple predictors. Transformations: linearizing curved data using log or power transformations. Vocabulary: scatterplot, regression, residual, correlation, outlier, influential point, leverage, least squares, coefficient of determination, transformation, linearity, residual plot.

skills.skill: Complex numbers (polar form, De Moivre's theorem)
skills.teks_description: Graph exponential, logarithmic, rational, polynomial, power, trigonometric, and piecewise functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers complex numbers in polar form for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(F)). Students should represent complex numbers in multiple forms and graph them. Rectangular form: z = a + bi. Polar (trigonometric) form: z = r(cos θ + i sin θ) = r·cis θ, where r = |z| = √(a²+b²) is the modulus and θ = arg(z) = tan^(-1)(b/a) is the argument. Euler's form: z = re^(iθ). Converting: a = r cos θ, b = r sin θ. Multiplication in polar form: z1·z2 = r1r2 · cis(θ1+θ2) — multiply moduli, add arguments. Division: z1/z2 = (r1/r2) · cis(θ1-θ2). De Moivre's theorem: z^n = r^n · cis(nθ); powerful for computing powers. nth roots: z^(1/n) = r^(1/n) · cis((θ+2πk)/n) for k = 0,1,...,n-1; produces n equally-spaced roots on a circle. Applications: roots of unity, signal processing, electrical engineering (impedance). Vocabulary: complex number, modulus, argument, polar form, rectangular form, De Moivre's theorem, roots of unity, Euler's formula, complex plane, Argand diagram.

skills.teks_description: Graph a set of parametric equations
skills.prompt: This topic covers parametric equations for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.3(A)). Students should graph sets of parametric equations and understand their applications. Parametric equations: define x and y separately as functions of a parameter t: x = f(t), y = g(t). Graphing: create table of t, x, y values; plot (x,y) points; indicate direction with arrows as t increases. Eliminating the parameter: solve one equation for t, substitute into the other to get Cartesian equation. Common parametric curves: line (x = x0+at, y = y0+bt), circle (x = r cos t, y = r sin t), ellipse (x = a cos t, y = b sin t), parabola. Applications: projectile motion (x = v0 cos θ · t, y = v0 sin θ · t - ½gt²), cycloids, planetary orbits, animation paths. Derivatives: dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt), giving slope of tangent to parametric curve. Key concepts: same Cartesian curve can have multiple parametric representations, parameter often represents time, direction of traversal matters. Vocabulary: parametric equation, parameter, Cartesian equation, eliminate the parameter, direction of traversal, cycloid, projectile, curve, arc length.

skills.teks_description: Graph points in the polar coordinate system and convert between rectangular and polar coordinates
skills.prompt: This topic covers polar coordinates for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.3(D)). Students should graph points and convert between rectangular and polar coordinate systems. Polar coordinates: point represented as (r, θ) where r is distance from origin (pole) and θ is angle from positive x-axis (polar axis). Conversions: x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ (polar to rectangular); r = √(x²+y²), θ = tan^(-1)(y/x) (rectangular to polar). Multiple representations: same point can be (r, θ), (r, θ+2π), (-r, θ+π). Common polar curves: circles (r = a, r = a cos θ, r = a sin θ), cardioids (r = a + a cos θ), limacons (r = a + b cos θ), roses (r = a cos(nθ) — n petals if n odd, 2n if even), spirals (r = aθ), lemniscates (r² = a² cos(2θ)). Graphing: use table of θ and r values, identify symmetry (about polar axis, about θ = π/2, about pole). Key concepts: converting equations between coordinate systems, identifying curve types, finding intersections of polar curves (set equations equal and solve, also check r = 0). Vocabulary: polar coordinates, pole, polar axis, rectangular coordinates, cardioid, limacon, rose curve, lemniscate, spiral, symmetry, conversion.
